You are currently advising on Individual tax strategies.
What is the Self Employed Health Insurance tax strategy and how does it work?

The Self Employed Health Insurance tax strategy allows taxpayers to The self-employed health insurance deduction can help offset some of the costs of paying out-of-pocket for health care coverage for you, your spouse, your dependents, and children under age 27.

You are AG FinTax Tax AI, an expert tax planning assistant for AG FinTax (Anil Grandhi's firm). You provide accurate, detailed tax strategy guidance based on IRS rules and professional tax advisory best practices. Always recommend consulting with a qualified tax advisor for implementation. Provide specific IRS form numbers, deadlines, and qualification criteria when relevant.
You are currently advising on Individual tax strategies.
Who qualifies for the Self Employed Health Insurance strategy? What are the eligibility requirements?

To qualify for the Self Employed Health Insurance strategy, you must meet the following requirements:
- Do you meet the definition of self employed? You meet the definition of self employed if you (1) carry on a trade or business as a sole proprietor or an independent contractor, (2) are a member of a partnership that carries on a trade or business, (3) are a member of an LLC classified as a partnership for tax purposes, or (4) are a shareholder of 2% or more of the stock of an S-Corp.
- Did you have earned income from your business? The Self Employed Health Insurance deduction cannot exceed the earned income from your business.
- Were you, your spouse, or any of your dependents eligible to participate in an employer-subsidized health plan for any month that you paid your premium? The self employed health insurance deduction is only available for months when neither you nor your spouse nor your dependents were eligible to participate in an employer-subsidized health plan.
- Did you pay any of the following premiums for yourself, your spouse, your dependents, and your children under age 27 even if they were not dependents: (1) medical insurance, (2) dental and vision insurance, (3) qualified long-term care insurance, or (4) Medicare? If you qualify, you will be able to deduct 100% of your premiums for health insurance, dental and vision insurance, and Medicare. There are limited deductions for qualified long-term care insurance.

What are the step-by-step action items to implement the Self Employed Health Insurance strategy?

Here are the required steps to implement the Self Employed Health Insurance strategy:
1. Purchase health insurance through the individual health insurance market or through the federal or state exchange marketplace (Mandatory)
2. If you are a more-than-2% shareholder of an S-Corp, and the S-Corp pays for the health insurance premiums, make sure that the S-Corp includes the premiums in income on your W-2. (Mandatory)

What is the Itemized Deduction tax strategy and how does it work?
The Itemized Deduction tax strategy allows taxpayers to Take itemized tax deductions on your tax return to reduce taxable income.

You are AG FinTax Tax AI, an expert tax planning assistant for AG FinTax (Anil Grandhi's firm). You provide accurate, detailed tax strategy guidance based on IRS rules and professional tax advisory best practices. Always recommend consulting with a qualified tax advisor for implementation. Provide specific IRS form numbers, deadlines, and qualification criteria when relevant.
You are currently advising on Individual tax strategies.
Who qualifies for the Itemized Deduction strategy? What are the eligibility requirements?

To qualify for the Itemized Deduction strategy, you must meet the following requirements:
- Do you have large unreimbursed medical and dental expenses? If you have incurred qualified out-of-pocket medical or dental expenses that are not covered by insurance, you may be able to deduct those expenses from your taxable income. The deduction for medical and dental expenses is limited to 7.5% of adjusted gross income.
- Do you pay a large amount of mortgage interest or real property taxes on your home? If you paid interest on a home mortgage or home equity line of credit, or paid real property taxes on your home, you may be able to deduct those expenses from your taxable income. The total of the state and local income tax deduction is limited to $10,000.
- Do you have large uninsured casualty or theft losses from a Federally declared disaster? If you lost your home, vehicles, or other personal property in a Federally declared disaster, you may be able to deduct the value of those losses from your taxable income. Personal casualty and theft losses from a federally delared disaster are subject to a $500/casualty limitation.
- Do you pay state and local income, sales, or personal property taxes? If you paid a large amount of local taxes, including state income taxes, and state sales taxes, you may be able to deduct those expenses from your taxable income. The total of the state and local income tax deduction is limited to $10,000.
- Do you make large contributions to charities? If you made large contributions to a qualified charity, you may be able to deduct those expenses from your taxable income.
- Are your itemized deductions greater than the standard deduction? If your itemized deductions are greater than the standard deduction, you will qualify to take the itemized deduction to reduce your taxable income.
- Is there any reason that you are prohibited from taking the standard deduction? If your itemized deductions are less than the standard deduction, but you are prohibited from taking the standard deduction, you may take the itemized deduction. You cannot take the standard deduction if (1) you are a married individual filing as married filing separately whose spouse itemizes deductions, (2) you are an individual who files a tax return for a period of less than 12 months because of a change in your annual accounting period, or (3) you are an individual who was a nonresident alien or a dual-status alien during the year and did not choose to be treated as a U.S. resident for tax purposes while married to a U.S. Citizen or alien resident.

What are the step-by-step action items to implement the Itemized Deduction strategy?

Here are the required steps to implement the Itemized Deduction strategy:
1. Document unreimbursed medical and dental expenses (Optional)
2. Pay interest to a mortgage holder. Obtain IRS Form 1098 (Optional)
3. Pay taxes on your real property to local tax jurisdicition (Optional)
4. Document unreimbursed casualty or theft losses from a federally declared disaster. If you had insurance, file an insurance claim and reduce the deduction by the amount of the insurance reimbursement. (Mandatory)
5. Obtain the disaster declaration number assigned by FEMA (Mandatory)
6. Obtain a qualified appraisal to determine the fair market value of property affected by a federally declared disaster or theft (Mandatory)
7. Make and document contributions to qualified charitable organizations (Optional)
8. Include gambling winnings as earned income that income can be reduced by applicable gambling losses. (Optional)

- Individual - 1040 or 1040-SR: 1040 or 1040SR - Schedule A | 2022-01-17 00:00:00 | Pub. 51 | Tax Advisor
- Individual - 1040 or 1040-SR: 1040 or 1040SR - Form 8283 | Tax Advisor | Required if noncash charitable contributions are over $500
- Individual - 1040 or 1040-SR: 1040 or 1040SR - Form 4684 | Tax Advisor | Required for casualty and theft losses from a federally declared disaster

Are there any state-specific considerations for the Itemized Deduction strategy?

Most states allow itemized deductions to determine state taxable income. Some states only allow the taxpayer to itemize deductions if they itemized on their federal return. Other states do not recognize federal itemized deductions but allow state-specific itemzied deductions. Lastly, other states do not allow any itemized deductions.

What is the Child Tax Credit tax strategy and how does it work?
The Child Tax Credit tax strategy allows taxpayers to This tax credit may help you with the cost of raising kids.

Who qualifies for the Child Tax Credit strategy? What are the eligibility requirements?

To qualify for the Child Tax Credit strategy, you must meet the following requirements:
- Will each child be under age 17 at the end of the tax year?  To be a qualifying child, your dependent must be under age 17 at the end of the tax year.
- Is each child your son, daughter, stepchild, eligible foster child, brother, sister, stepbrother, stepsister, half-brother, half-sister, or a descendant of one of these (for example, a grandchild, niece or nephew)?
- Did you provide at least half of the financial support during the year for each child?  To qualify, each child cannot have provided more than half of his or her own financial support during the tax year.
- Has each child lived with you more than half of the year?  To qualify, each child must live with you in the U.S. for more than half the year. The time that the child lives with you does not have to be consecutive.
- Can each child be claimed as a dependent on your tax return?  Each child must be considered a dependent for tax filing purposes.
- Does each child have a Social Security number that is valid for employment?  Each child must have a valid Social Security number.
- Is each child a U.S. citizen, a U.S. National, or a U.S. resident alien?
- Do you meet the income test?  You qualify for the full amount of the tax credit if your annual income is not more than $2000,000 for single filers or $400,000 for joint filers.

State Child Tax Credits as of July 2022: The following twelve states have enacted a child tax credit in addition to the federal credit: California, Colorado, Connecticut, Idaho, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, Oklahoma and Vermont. Source: National Conference of State Legislatures at (https://www.ncsl.org/research/human-services/child-tax-credit-overview.aspx#:~:text=Nine%20of%20the%20twelve%20states,limit%20for%20the%20tax%20credit).

What is the Coverdell ESA tax strategy and how does it work?

The Coverdell ESA tax strategy allows taxpayers to You can save for your child's education while having tax-deferred growth of your assets and having tax-free distributions for qualified educational expenses.

Who qualifies for the Coverdell ESA strategy? What are the eligibility requirements?

To qualify for the Coverdell ESA strategy, you must meet the following requirements:
- Is the child under 18 years old? When the account is established, the child who will be the designated beneficiary of the Coverdell ESA must be under 18 years old. There can be exceptions to this rule if the child has special needs.
- Will the contributions to the account be $2,000 or less per child per year? You can have more than one Coverdell account for a child, but the total contribution to all accounts for that child cannot exceed $2,000 per year, even if made by different contributors.
- Is your modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) less than $95,000 per year, if filing as a single, or less than $190,000 per year, if married filing jointly? If your income is within these limits, you can contribute up to $2,000 into the Coverdell account. If your MAGI is above $110,000 as a single filer, or above $220,000 per year, if married filing jointly, you are not eligible to contribute to a Coverdell account. If your income falls between the lower and higher limits, the amount that you can contribute will be reduced. There are no income limits on organizations, such as corporations or trusts.
- Do you plan on either distributing the funds in the Coverdell account by the time the child is 30 or rolling the account over to another child? If the child does not go to college by age 30 and does not use all of the funds for qualified education expenses, the funds will be forcibly distributed with a 10% penalty, and income tax will come due at that time. To avoid a penalty, the beneficiary of the account can be changed to someone under age 30 or the funds can be transferred to a Section 529 plan. In the event that the distributions are higher than the expenses, the gains are taxed at the beneficiary's rate, rather than the contributor's rate.
- Will the educational expenses for which you want the funds used be considered qualified expenses? Qualified educational expenses include, but are not limited to: tuition, room and board, computers and laptops even if not required, books and supplies, tutoring, and transportation. The IRS takes a broad view of what are considered qualified educational expenses.

What are the step-by-step action items to implement the Coverdell ESA strategy?

Here are the required steps to implement the Coverdell ESA strategy:
1. Set up the Coverdell ESA at a financial institution before the child turns 18 years old. The account must be designated as a Coverdell ESA when it is set up. Enrollment forms will need to be completed, including designating a beneficiary and a "responsible individual. (Mandatory)
2. Make a cash contribution into the Coverdell ESA. The cash contribution can be invested in any qualifying investment (not life insurance) available at the financial institution where the ESA is set up. (Mandatory)

The Dependent Care Credit tax strategy allows taxpayers to This tax credit may help you pay for the care of eligible dependents.

Who qualifies for the Dependent Care Credit strategy? What are the eligibility requirements?

To qualify for the Dependent Care Credit strategy, you must meet the following requirements:
- Do you pay someone to care for a dependent who lives with you so that you can go to work, look for work, or go to school? If you pay someone to care for a dependent who is a “qualifying person” so that you can go to work, look for work, or go to school, you may be eligible to claim the Dependent Care tax credit.
- Is the dependent who is receiving care under age 13?
- Is the dependent your spouse, and is your spouse not able to mentally or physically care for themself, and did your spouse live in your home at least half of the year?
- Is the dependent someone other than your child or spouse, and is the person not able to take care of himself or herself, and has the person lived in your home at least half of the year?
- Did you or your spouse or do you or your spouse earn income from a job during the period that care was provided or is being provided?
- Is you and your spouse's combined earned income less than $438,000 per year?

Contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free — a triple tax benefit.

What is the Donor-Advised Fund tax strategy and how does it work?

A charitable giving vehicle that provides an immediate tax deduction when contributions are made, while allowing the donor to recommend grants to charities over time. Ideal for bunching charitable deductions.

What is the Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD) tax strategy and how does it work?

Allows individuals aged 70½ or older to transfer up to $100,000 per year directly from an IRA to a qualified charity. The distribution is excluded from taxable income and counts toward the required minimum distribution (RMD).

What is the Qualified Opportunity Zone tax strategy and how does it work?

Invest capital gains into a Qualified Opportunity Zone Fund to defer and potentially reduce capital gains taxes. If the investment is held for 10 or more years, any appreciation in the investment is tax-free.

What is the Tax Loss Harvesting tax strategy and how does it work?

Sell investments that have declined in value to offset capital gains from other investments. Net losses can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year, with excess losses carried forward to future years.

What is the Sale of Home Gain Exclusion tax strategy and how does it work?

Exclude up to $250,000 ($500,000 for married filing jointly) of capital gain from the sale of a primary residence. Must have owned and used the home as a primary residence for at least 2 of the 5 years before the sale.

What are the differences between S Corporation, C Corporation, Partnership, and Schedule C entity elections?

Each entity type has distinct tax implications:

- Schedule C (Sole Proprietorship): Simplest structure. Income reported on personal return. Subject to self-employment tax on all net income.

- Partnership: For businesses with 2+ owners. Income passes through to partners. Can save on self-employment tax through proper allocation of guaranteed payments vs. distributive shares.

- S Corporation: Limits shareholders to 100 US residents. Income above reasonable compensation is exempt from self-employment tax. Requires payroll setup and Form 1120-S filing.

- C Corporation: Separate tax entity. Subject to corporate tax rates. Dividends to shareholders are taxed again (double taxation). Best for businesses planning to reinvest profits.

- Late S Election: Retroactive S Corp election if you missed the deadline. Must file within 3 years and 75 days of the effective date.

What retirement plan options are available for small business owners and which is best?

Small business owners have several retirement plan options:

- Solo 401(k): Best for self-employed with no employees. Highest contribution limits with both employee and employer contributions.
- SIMPLE IRA: Easy to set up for businesses with 100 or fewer employees. Lower contribution limits but minimal administration.
- SIMPLE 401(k): Combines SIMPLE IRA ease with 401(k) features.
- Profit Sharing Plan: Flexible employer contributions that can vary year to year.
- Cash Balance Plan: Allows significantly higher contributions, especially for older owners.
- Defined Benefit Plan: Highest possible contributions. Best for high-income owners near retirement.

The best plan depends on your age, income, number of employees, and how much you want to contribute. Older, high-income owners benefit most from Defined Benefit or Cash Balance plans.

What deduction strategies can help reduce my business's taxable income?

Key business deduction strategies include:

- Augusta Rule: Rent your home to your business up to 14 days/year tax-free.
- Business Meals: 50% deductible for business-purpose meals at restaurants.
- Travel Expenses: Deduct business travel costs including transportation, lodging, and incidentals.
- Accountable Plan: Tax-free reimbursement to employees for business expenses.
- Home Office: Deduct a portion of home expenses based on business use percentage.
- Section 179 Expensing: Immediately deduct the full cost of qualifying equipment.
- Bonus Depreciation: Deduct a large percentage of asset costs in the first year.
- Cost Segregation: Accelerate depreciation on commercial real estate components.

What tax strategies involve family members?

Family-related tax strategies include:

- Hiring Children: Employ children in your business to shift income to lower brackets. Under 18: exempt from Social Security and Medicare. Under 21: exempt from FUTA.
- Family Office Management Company: Create a separate entity to provide financial services to family businesses, employing family members at fair market wages.
- Child Tax Credit: Up to $2,000 per qualifying child under 17.
- Dependent Care Credit: Credit for care of dependents under 13 so you can work.
- Coverdell ESA: Tax-deferred education savings up to $2,000/year per child.
- Qualified Education Assistance Program: Employer-provided up to $5,250/year tax-free for education.
